Omega-3 oil from Antarctic krill (Euphausia superba). EPA and DHA are primarily phospholipid-bound, yielding higher bioavailability than triglyceride-form fish oil — but absorption still requires dietary fat; take with a fat-containing meal. Typical dose 500–2000 mg/day (providing ~150–500 mg EPA+DHA). Mild platelet-inhibiting and blood-thinning effect at higher doses — caution with concurrent fibrinolytic/anticoagulant supplements (e.g., Nattokinase). Sources: examine.com (Krill Oil); Ulven & Holven 2015, Nutrients; NIH ODS (Omega-3 Fatty Acids).

Acetylated form of L-carnitine; crosses the blood-brain barrier unlike plain L-carnitine. Dual action: mitochondrial support (shuttles acetyl groups into the TCA cycle) and cholinergic modulation (donates acetyl group for ACh synthesis). Typical dose 500–2000 mg/day, morning or midday on an empty stomach. TMAO production via gut microbiome warrants caution in cardiovascular risk profiles. Sources: examine.com (Acetyl-L-Carnitine), Malaguarnera et al. 2007 (Am J Clin Nutr).

L-tartrate salt form of L-carnitine; does NOT cross the blood-brain barrier — no nootropic or central stimulant action (contrast: ALCAR). Mechanism is peripheral: replenishes muscle carnitine stores, supports mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation, and reduces exercise-induced oxidative damage/DOMS. Typical dose 1–3 g/day, commonly pre- or post-workout on empty stomach for absorption. TMAO production via gut microbiome is a shared concern with all L-carnitine forms; caution in cardiovascular risk profiles. Sources: examine.com (L-Carnitine L-Tartrate), Kraemer et al. 2003 (J Strength Cond Res).

Prescription drug in Russia, banned as supplement in US — regulatory status has no dedicated trait

Synthetic conjugate of GABA and niacin (nicotinic acid), developed in the USSR. Upon absorption it hydrolyzes into GABA and niacin; niacin acts as a vasodilator and may lower blood pressure. Typical Russian prescription dose 20–50 mg 2–3x/day. Classified as a prescription drug in Russia (NPK ECHO); banned from US dietary supplements by FDA in 2015 (not a lawful dietary ingredient under DSHEA). A 2023 assay study found picamilon itself inactive against 50 biological targets including GABA receptors (PubMed 36668678), leaving mechanism partially contested. Blood pressure effects via niacin moiety warrant caution in hypotensive individuals. Sources: Wikipedia (Picamilon); FDA constituent update Nov 2015; PubMed 36668678.

Water-soluble potassium salt used for general K supplementation, urinary alkalinization, and kidney-stone prevention (raises urinary pH, reduces uric acid/oxalate crystallization). NOW Foods label reports 99 mg elemental potassium from 310 mg potassium citrate. Typical supplemental dose 99–1000 mg elemental K/day, often split across meals to reduce GI irritation (nausea, stomach discomfort common on empty stomach). Significant interactions: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, K-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, triamterene, amiloride), NSAIDs — all raise hyperkalemia risk. Sources: NIH ODS (Potassium Fact Sheet); examine.com (Potassium Citrate); NOW Foods product page.
